topics.headline: Newsom's May Revise puts $5B on the table for "Student Support and Professional Development" — and the rules of engagement for charters just changed
topics.category: A (state K-12 funding) — strongest candidate from the policy_funding_hawk lens
topics.why_matters: This is the largest discretionary intervention-eligible block grant California has put forward in a generation, and it lands at exactly the moment charters are setting 2026-27 LCAPs. The revised funding estimate for Proposition 98, the 40% of the state general fund that must go to TK-12 and community colleges, would be a record $127.1 billion in 2026-27, with per student funding increasing to a record $21,013 per pupil. Two specific signals matter for A+'s partner base: The Governor's revised budget sets a record-high level of education spending. The Governor's proposal includes new significant funding through a $5 billion Student Support and Professional Development Discretionary Block Grant. And separately, the May Revise proposes new requirements for charter schools to address verified data, fraud, and Assistance Center oversight — a compliance signal worth flagging early to NCB partners.
topics.angle: Translate the $5B block grant into plain language for charter intervention coordinators — "Here's what this means for your 2026-27 LCAP, your MTSS budget, and your intervention RFPs."
topics.roman_take: Conviction angle — discretionary block grants are only as good as the evidence schools use to spend them; absent ESSA Tier 1/2 evidence requirements at the state level, the $5B becomes another round of "buy a thing, hope it works." Push the audience to spend like Title I auditors are watching.
topics.danielle_take: Implementation angle — walk a charter director through the three-meeting sequence (May Revise → June 15 Legislature deadline → July 1 final) and what to put in the LCAP draft *now* so the block grant funds intervention rather than line-item drift. ---

topics.headline: The May 31 ELPAC reclassification deadline just passed — and most charters left RFEPs on the table
topics.category: B (acute K-12 problem story) with direct C overlay (hits every Tier A partner with ELs)
topics.why_matters: English learner students not reclassified by May 31, 2026 can no longer be reclassified based on 2024−25 Summative ELPAC/Alternate ELPAC scores, and must be reclassified based on 2025−26 scores. That window closed four days ago. Simultaneously, researchers from Stanford University worked with nine districts and found that 39% of students in those districts who scored 4 on the ELPAC were not reclassified the next year, and in 2021-22 and 2022-23, about 18,500 students statewide annually had the highest possible score on the English Language Proficiency Assessment for California but remained classified as English learners. This is A+'s LTEL reclassification proof point as a state-level emergency — and it hit every partner with an EL roster this week. Assessing less than 95 percent of current EL students negatively impacts LEAs' ELPI on the Dashboard.
topics.angle: "May 31 just passed. Here's what every charter director needs to check in their CALPADS extract this week — and the LTEL reclassification gap A+ has closed for partners like Ocean Grove."
topics.roman_take: A blunt take on why "compliance deadline" framing buries the real story — schools aren't failing the deadline because of paperwork, they're failing it because the reclassification system itself was designed to keep kids in the EL category.
topics.danielle_take: The implementation playbook — what a charter's intervention coordinator should be doing in the next 30 days to ensure 2025–26 ELPAC results get acted on instead of sitting until next May. ---

topics.headline: The ESSER-to-State "Market Reset" — and why NSSA-style quality screens favor embedded providers like A+
topics.why_matters: The number of public schools providing high-dosage tutoring grew by 5% between 2024 and 2025 according to NCES, and many districts are holding onto tutoring as a core academic intervention, particularly those that saw it as more than a short-term fix during the pandemic. The story is no longer "will tutoring survive ESSER?" — it's "which providers survive the state-level quality screen?" States doing state-level contracting haven't always gone with providers that have the strongest evidence base, and the National Student Support Accelerator's badging system for tutoring providers is being positioned as a quick way for states to prioritize providers whose practices match effectiveness — though most states currently rely on vendors self-reporting attributes. Meanwhile, FutureEd is tracking 41 bills across 19 states in the 2026 session aimed at sustaining tutoring, with most focused on programs with four or fewer students working with the same tutor for at least 30 minutes per session, three times a week, over several months. A+'s LTEL small-group model already meets that statutory definition — most national vendors don't.
topics.angle: "The K-12 tutoring market just split in two — and the quality screen states are adopting was written for providers who already operate like us."
topics.roman_take: Self-reported vendor attributes are how you end up with $33M state contracts going to apps. Conviction angle: stop letting vendors grade their own homework — make NSSA badging the floor, not the ceiling.
topics.danielle_take: Walk charter directors through what a state-level preferred-provider list will actually look like for them in 2026-27, and what intake/scheduling documentation they need on file *now* to qualify when CARS or state-tutoring grants come down. ---
